section.title: The Sanctity of Blood
section.sectionRationale: This opens the Holiness Code, chapters 17 through 26, by centralizing all Israelite slaughter and sacrifice at the tabernacle and forbidding the eating of blood, grounding both commands in the single truth that life itself is in the blood.
section.thesis: God centralizes all Israelite slaughter and sacrifice at the tabernacle and forbids eating blood, because blood carries life itself, and He alone has appointed it to make atonement on the altar.
section.frameNote: Three movements: sacrifices must be brought to the tabernacle, not offered in the open field to goat demons; blood must never be eaten, because life is in the blood and it is given for atonement; and the same rule binds native Israelite and resident foreigner alike.

units.label: One place of sacrifice
units.note: no more offerings in the open field

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Speak to Aaron, his sons, and all the Israelites and tell them this is what the LORD has commanded:
‘Anyone from the house of Israel who slaughters an ox, a lamb, or a goat in the camp or outside of it

instead of bringing it to the entrance to the Tent of Meeting to present it as an offering to the LORD before His tabernacle, that man shall incur bloodguilt. He has shed blood and must be cut off from among his people.

For this reason the Israelites will bring to the LORD the sacrifices they have been offering in the open fields. They are to bring them to the priest at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting and offer them as sacrifices of peace to the LORD.
The priest will then sprinkle the blood on the altar of the LORD at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting and burn the fat as a pleasing aroma to the LORD.
They must no longer offer their sacrifices to the {{seirim|goat demons}} to which they have prostituted themselves. This will be a permanent statute for them for the generations to come.’
Tell them that if anyone from the house of Israel or any foreigner living among them offers a burnt offering or a sacrifice
but does not bring it to the entrance to the Tent of Meeting to sacrifice it to the LORD, that man must be cut off from his people.
If anyone from the house of Israel or a foreigner living among them eats any blood, I will set My face against that person and cut him off from among his people.

For the {{nephesh|life}} of the flesh is in the {{dam|blood}}, and I have given it to you to make {{kipper|atonement}} for your souls upon the altar; for it is the blood that makes atonement for the soul.
Therefore I say to the Israelites, ‘None of you may eat blood, nor may any foreigner living among you eat blood.’
And if any Israelite or foreigner living among them hunts down a wild animal or bird that may be eaten, he must drain its blood and cover it with dirt.

For the life of all flesh is its blood. Therefore I have told the Israelites, ‘You must not eat the blood of any living thing, because the life of all flesh is its blood; whoever eats it must be cut off.’

units.label: One law for native and foreigner

verses.text: And any person, whether native or foreigner, who eats anything found dead or mauled by wild beasts must wash his clothes and bathe with water, and he will be unclean until evening; then he will be clean.
But if he does not wash his clothes and bathe himself, then he shall bear his iniquity.”

seirim.gloss: Literally goats, hairy ones; here referring to goat idols or demonic beings Israel had apparently sacrificed to in Egypt or the wilderness. By requiring all sacrifice at the tabernacle, God cuts off this hidden, illegitimate worship at the root.

nephesh.gloss: Life, soul, or living being. The word describes the whole animated creature, not a separable inner part; here it identifies blood as the vehicle of that very life, which is why it belongs to God alone and is never ordinary food.

dam.gloss: Blood. Central to this chapter's logic: blood is not merely a substance but the visible bearer of nephesh, life, which is why God reserves it exclusively for atonement on the altar and forbids its consumption.

kipper.gloss: To atone, to cover. Verse 11 gives the theological center of Leviticus's whole sacrificial system: God Himself has given the life bearing blood to Israel specifically so that it can make atonement for their souls.

basic.context: This law requires every animal slaughtered for sacrifice to be brought to the tabernacle rather than offered informally in the open country, and it forbids eating blood altogether, whether from a sacrifice or from hunted game.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a ceremonial and civil law for Israel's old covenant camp, not a rule binding Christians today, who are told food itself does not defile, Mark 7:19. Its abiding truth, restated in the New Testament, is the seriousness of blood as the marker of life and atonement.
basic.originalLanguages: Nephesh, life or soul, and dam, blood, are tied tightly together in verse 11: blood is not simply a fluid but the visible bearer of the creature's very life, which is why God assigns it, and it alone, to make kipper, atonement, on the altar.
basic.typology: Blood given by God for atonement anticipates the blood of Christ, given once for all; the single place of sacrifice anticipates the truth that there is only one mediator and one altar of the cross through whom sinners approach God.
basic.christ: Hebrews 9:22 draws directly on this chapter's principle: without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness. Christ's own blood, poured out once, accomplishes what all this centralized, repeated bloodshed could only foreshadow, securing an eternal, not merely annual, atonement.
basic.god: God claims exclusive right over blood because He is the giver of life itself; He does not allow Israel to treat life carelessly, whether by casual slaughter divorced from His altar or by consuming the very substance He has reserved for atonement.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 9:22 states that nearly everything is purified with blood, and without shedding of blood there is no forgiveness. Acts 15:20, 29 carries this concern about blood into the early church's guidance for Gentile believers. Genesis 9:4 already forbids eating blood at the flood covenant, showing this principle predates Sinai.
basic.oneStory: From Noah onward, Scripture treats blood as sacred because it carries life, and life belongs to God. This chapter concentrates that principle at Israel's one altar, preparing the way for the truth that all true atonement flows from one place and one blood, the blood of Christ shed once for all.

context.literary: This chapter opens the Holiness Code, chapters 17 through 26, moving from the priestly rituals of chapters 1 through 16 into laws addressed to the whole community, beginning with the most basic act underlying all sacrifice: the shedding of blood.
context.historical: In the wilderness, with the tabernacle at the center of the camp, requiring all slaughter to pass through the priesthood prevented the informal, scattered worship, including sacrifice to goat demons, that had apparently crept in during Israel's time in Egypt.
context.canonical: This chapter's principle, life is in the blood, becomes foundational to the whole biblical theology of atonement, cited directly in Hebrews and echoed in the early church's guidance to Gentile believers in Acts.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To centralize Israel's sacrificial worship entirely at the tabernacle, cutting off hidden idolatrous slaughter, and to ground the prohibition on eating blood in the theological truth that blood carries life and is given by God specifically for atonement.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage prescribes specific old covenant regulations, centralized sacrifice and a total ban on eating blood, that governed Israel's camp; it does not bind Christians to a single physical altar or forbid rare meat as such, since the New Testament clarifies that food itself does not defile a person, Mark 7:19. Its enduring truth is the sanctity of blood as the marker of life and the means of atonement, fulfilled in Christ.

debates.question: Does the prohibition on eating blood in Acts 15:20, 29 mean this Levitical law still binds Christians today?

views.label: A temporary accommodation for Jewish and Gentile fellowship
views.case: Many read the Jerusalem council's decree as a practical measure to preserve unity between Jewish and Gentile believers in mixed congregations, not a reassertion of Mosaic law as such, since the same council explicitly frees Gentiles from circumcision and the broader law.

views.label: A creational principle predating Sinai
views.case: Others note the blood prohibition originates at Genesis 9:4, before the Mosaic covenant, and see its echo in Acts as evidence that the underlying principle, reverence for blood as life, still carries some abiding weight even under the new covenant.

debates.bearing: Both views agree the church is not bound to Israel's centralized altar system or its full dietary code; they differ chiefly on how much weight the Genesis 9 principle itself still carries.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's key claim rests on the tight pairing of nephesh, life or soul, and dam, blood, in verse 11: the flesh's very life resides in its blood, so God assigns blood, and only blood, to make kipper, atonement, on the altar. The se'irim, goat demons, named in verse 7 point to an otherwise unrecorded lapse into idolatrous sacrifice this law is designed to end.

typology.type: One altar, one place of sacrifice
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the truth that there is one mediator between God and men, and one sufficient sacrifice, the cross of Christ, 1 Timothy 2:5, Hebrews 10:12.

typology.type: Blood given by God specifically for atonement
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the blood of Christ, given once for all, which obtains eternal redemption rather than a repeated, yearly covering, Hebrews 9:12, 22.

study.christ: Verse 11 states the principle Hebrews 9:22 later makes explicit: without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness. Every animal's blood brought to this one altar rehearsed a truth completed in Christ, whose own blood, poured out once at the cross, secures an eternal redemption that the whole apparatus of centralized, repeated sacrifice could only foreshadow, Hebrews 9:12.
study.god: God claims sole right over blood because He alone is the giver of life; this is not squeamishness but theology. He will not let Israel either shed blood carelessly, divorced from His altar and His appointed mercy, or treat blood as ordinary food, since it is the very thing He has set apart to atone for sin.

crossReferences.note: God forbids eating blood with the flesh at the covenant with Noah, showing this principle predates the Sinai law.
Nearly everything is purified with blood, and without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins.
The Jerusalem council instructs Gentile believers to abstain from blood, carrying this concern into the early, mixed church.

study.oneStory: From Noah to Sinai to the cross, Scripture treats blood as sacred because it is the visible bearer of life, and life belongs to God. This chapter concentrates that principle at Israel's one altar, preparing the way for the truth completed in Christ: one blood, shed once, sufficient to atone for sin forever.

quiz.q: Where were Israelites required to bring any ox, lamb, or goat they slaughtered, according to this chapter?

options.text: To the entrance of the Tent of Meeting, to be offered to the LORD.
options.note: Verses 3-6 require this centralized offering.

options.text: To any local altar of their choosing.
options.note: The whole point of the law is to end scattered, local slaughter and centralize it at the tabernacle.

options.text: To the goat demons in the open field.
options.note: Verse 7 explicitly forbids this practice, which the law is correcting.

options.text: No location is specified.
options.note: Verses 3-6 specify the Tent of Meeting directly.

quiz.q: According to verse 11, why is blood given such significance in this chapter?

options.text: Because the life of the flesh is in the blood, and God gave it to make atonement on the altar.
options.note: Verse 11 states this reasoning explicitly.

options.text: Because blood was simply considered unappetizing.
options.note: The text gives a theological reason, life and atonement, not a matter of taste.

options.text: Because it was a local cultural taboo with no stated reason.
options.note: Verse 11 gives an explicit theological rationale.

options.text: No reason is given in the text.
options.note: Verse 11 explicitly explains the reasoning.

quiz.q: What was a hunter required to do with the blood of a wild animal or bird taken for food?

options.text: Drain its blood and cover it with dirt.
options.note: Verse 13 specifies this practice.

options.text: Bring the whole animal to the tabernacle to be sacrificed.
options.note: Verses 3-7 concern domesticated animals fit for sacrifice; verse 13 concerns wild game hunted for food, requiring only that its blood be drained and covered.

options.text: Drink the blood as part of the hunt.
options.note: Verses 10-14 explicitly forbid consuming any blood.

options.text: Nothing specific was required.
options.note: Verse 13 gives a specific instruction.

quiz.q: To whom did the prohibition on eating blood apply?

options.text: Both native Israelites and foreigners living among them.
options.note: Verses 10, 12, and 13 explicitly include the foreign resident.

options.text: Only native born Israelites.
options.note: The text explicitly extends the law to foreigners living among Israel as well.

options.text: Only the priests.
options.note: The prohibition applies to the whole community, not priests alone.

options.text: No group is specified.
options.note: Verses 10-13 specify both native and foreigner directly.

quiz.q: How does Hebrews 9:22 pick up this chapter's principle about blood?

options.text: It states that without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins.
options.note: Hebrews 9:22 draws this conclusion directly from the Levitical logic of blood and atonement.

options.text: It rejects the idea that blood has any connection to forgiveness.
options.note: Hebrews 9:22 affirms, rather than rejects, this connection between blood and forgiveness.

options.text: It applies this only to animal sacrifices, never to Christ.
options.note: Hebrews 9 explicitly applies this principle to Christ's own blood as the greater fulfillment.

options.text: Hebrews never references this principle.
options.note: Hebrews 9:22 draws on it directly.

questions: This law refused to let Israel treat sacrifice as something private and scattered, apart from God's appointed place. Where might you be tempted to approach God on your own terms rather than through Christ, His one appointed way?
God reserved blood, the marker of life, exclusively for atonement. How does knowing that your forgiveness cost actual life, not a mere formality, shape how seriously you take your own sin?

Israel had apparently drifted into secret sacrifices to goat demons before this law corrected it. What smaller, half hidden compromises in your own worship or devotion need to be brought back into the light?

The same law bound both native Israelite and resident foreigner equally. Where do you need to extend the same grace and the same standards to those who are different from you, rather than a double standard?

Hebrews says without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness. Sit with the weight of that this week: what does it cost you to remember that your forgiveness was bought, not simply given for free?

translationNotes.note: Goat demons translates se'irim, literally hairy ones or goats; the term likely refers to goat shaped idols or demonic beings associated with wilderness or wasteland worship, practices Israel is here commanded to abandon entirely.

The life of the flesh is in the blood renders a construction that ties nephesh, life or soul, directly to dam, blood; the verse gives the theological rationale for the whole chapter's ban on consuming blood.

Cut off from among his people describes a penalty, karet in Hebrew, whose precise mechanism, divine judgment, execution, or exclusion, is debated among scholars, but whose severity is not: it marks the most serious category of covenant violation short of specific capital sentences elsewhere in the law.
